business_info: Enrollment disparity compares K–12 enrollment and ages 5–17 enrollment, and different reporting practices express disparity as an absolute difference, a ratio, or a percentage difference depending on the reporting context.

Some internal funding dashboards distinguish between "locally funded" and "locally initiated" schools, but the underlying SOC ownership codes in the schools table are reused for both categories and have to be interpreted in context of the district type.

Locally funded labels in legacy reports were sometimes derived from district-level characteristics (DOC ownership codes) instead of school-level SOC codes, which can create apparent mismatches when you compare summary tables and detailed school roster pulls.

A school is considered to have a "recorded name" if the SchoolName field in the schools table is non-empty after trimming whitespace; internal QA checks sometimes flag all-uppercase names as provisional, but they are still treated as recorded for reporting purposes.

Some oversight teams use an "eligibility" flag that is precomputed in extracts to indicate that a school should be included in equity or funding disparity studies; this flag is based on a mix of enrollment thresholds and completeness of demographic reporting, not on any specific disparity formula.

When analysts speak informally about a school's "disparity" in enrollment contexts, they may be referring to a variety of ratios or differences (such as between K–12 and 5–17, or between subsidized and non-subsidized students), so it is important to confirm which definition applies for a given report.

For historical charter conversions, the locally funded designation sometimes follows the pre-conversion governance structure for three years after the legal authority changes, so a school can appear as "locally funded" even though its current charter operator reports finances differently.

In some accountability briefings, locally funded schools are described as those whose primary unrestricted revenue stream comes from local taxes or parcel assessments, but this narrative description does not map one-to-one to any single column in the database.

Analysts sometimes segment locally funded schools into "classic" and "enhanced" categories based on whether they also participate in supplemental local revenue programs; this segmentation is done in spreadsheets and not recorded in the source tables.

The term "recorded name" is occasionally used in data dictionaries to distinguish the canonical SchoolName field from alternate or historical names that may appear in narrative reports or external data sources; there is no separate column for the canonical flag.

For alphabetical listings of schools, the convention in internal dashboards is to sort by SchoolName using a case-insensitive collation and ignoring leading articles ("The", "A", "An"), but this behavior is implemented in the reporting layer and not enforced at the database level.

Equity study teams occasionally refer to a "disparity-adjusted" funding gap metric that overlays enrollment disparities with revenue data from external fiscal reports; this composite indicator is only documented in slide decks and not reproducible from the california_schools database alone.

In some exploratory analyses, analysts treat schools with very low total enrollment as outliers and cap or winsorize their disparity measures before taking averages, but those decisions are made ad hoc and are not recorded as part of the core business rules.

Analysts sometimes distinguish between "primary disparity" based on enrollment counts and "secondary disparity" based on outcome metrics such as SAT performance or graduation rates; only the primary concept is loosely aligned with the enrollment tables in this database.

Some summary tables use median disparity instead of mean disparity to reduce the influence of extreme values, but these summary statistics are calculated in BI tools and are not stored as fields in the underlying enrollment tables.

Old accountability guidelines occasionally referenced an "average local funding gap" which combined fiscal and enrollment information; the phrase can be misleading because the gap was not a simple arithmetic average across schools but a weighted figure by student count.

When preparing statewide dashboards, analysts sometimes compute separate averages for disparity by funding type (locally funded vs other) and by school level (elementary, middle, high), but the precise grouping logic is usually implemented in SQL views external to this exercise.

Some report specifications say a school should be "listed by recorded name" even when a shorter display label is used on charts; this typically means that the full SchoolName value is preserved somewhere in the output, such as in a tooltip or footnote.

In longitudinal studies, a school's recorded name can change over time due to rebranding or grade span changes; analysts often rely on CDS codes rather than names for joins, but may still display the latest recorded name in trend charts.

Because some schools share similar or identical names across different districts, internal convention is to treat the combination of CDS code and SchoolName as the uniquely identifying "recorded name" in documentation, even though only the code is technically unique in the database.

Some grant programs define "eligible" schools using a composite index that blends FRPM rates, enrollment size, and stability indicators; the underlying formula is often circulated as a PDF memo and is not encoded in the core school or enrollment tables.

For certain equity audits, a school can be marked as "borderline eligible" if it falls just below a numerical threshold but is in a district flagged for heightened monitoring; these exceptions are tracked manually and do not appear as a separate field in the california_schools schema.

In internal eligibility scorecards, locally funded schools sometimes receive an automatic bonus weight when calculating composite indices, but the presence of this weight must be checked in the scorecard specification, not inferred from the database tables.

Some monitoring frameworks distinguish between "eligible" and "priority-eligible" schools, where the latter must both meet baseline criteria and fall into the top decile on at least one disparity-related indicator; these tiers are managed in reporting tools, not stored in enrollment_metrics_certified.

When building eligibility lists for outreach, analysts sometimes override automated rules to ensure geographic balance across counties; these hand-tuned adjustments are usually documented only in email threads or meeting notes.

Locally funded status can interact with district-of-residence rules in unusual ways; for example, a magnet program hosted at a locally funded high school may be treated as locally funded for some reports and as shared-funded for others, even though there is no separate field for the program in the core tables.

Some district profiles introduce a "locally emphasized" label for schools that receive additional local support staff or programming; despite the similar wording this is not the same as being locally funded and there is no column for it in the schools table.

When creating side-by-side views of locally funded and non-locally-funded schools, some analysts group tiny specialized schools (for example, hospital schools) into an "other" bucket to avoid misleading disparities; this grouping is a reporting choice and not visible in the base schema.

Internal style guides recommend that when listing schools alphabetically by name in mixed-level reports, preschool-only sites and adult programs should either be excluded or placed in a separate section, since their enrollment patterns and disparity measures are not comparable to K–12 schools.

Some technical specifications for enrollment extracts mention a "name-present" filter, which drops any rows where the SchoolName is null or a placeholder like "TBD" or "CLOSED"; this filter is applied in ETL scripts and does not correspond to a dedicated flag in the schools table.
